---
title: "Using AI-generated images for scientific presentations and reports"
author: "Ricardo Oliveros-Ramos"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
execute:
  echo: false
---

::: {#fig-ai-guidelines-example fig-alt="Infographic showing a workflow for responsible use of AI-generated images in research: AI image → metadata → repository+DOI → CC BY 4.0 → slide/paper caption; includes a reminder to check third-party rights."}

![](AI-generated-images-science.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}

Workflow to document an AI-generated image for scientific use. *Source:* AI image (ChatGPT/OpenAI, 2026), CC BY 4.0. Zenodo: doi:10.5281/zenodo.XXXXXXX

:::

## 1) Minimum metadata to record

After creating the image, store this in a `README.txt` next to the image, or in the repository description.

- **Generator** (provider + product + model/version if known)  
  e.g., *ChatGPT Images (OpenAI)*; *Midjourney v6*; *Adobe Firefly*; *DreamStudio (Stability AI)*
- **Date generated** (YYYY-MM-DD)
- **Prompt** (final prompt; optionally key iterations)
- **Key settings** (if available): aspect ratio, seed, upscaler, negative prompt, etc.
- **Post-processing**: what was changed (crop, colour, labels, compositing) + software used
- **Intended use**: decorative/intro illustration vs scientific/interpretive figure

## 2) Rights and reuse: what you typically get from common AI-image generators

These are *contractual* rights from the provider’s terms; they are separate from whether copyright exists in your jurisdiction.

### ChatGPT / OpenAI (images generated in ChatGPT)

- As between you and OpenAI, you **own the Output** and OpenAI assigns its rights (if any) to you, to the extent permitted by law.  
  Source: OpenAI Terms of Use.

### Midjourney

- Midjourney states you **own the images/videos you create** and can use them commercially, with exceptions/conditions described in their documentation/terms.  
  Source: Midjourney documentation on using images/videos commercially.

> **Good practice:** if using Midjourney, confirm your account/subscription conditions at the time of creation and record a screenshot or PDF of the relevant terms page.

### BananaProAI

- BananaProAI states that **users own the images generated by the system** and may use them **freely, including for commercial purposes**, subject to the platform’s Terms of Service.

**Good practice:**  
Record the tool name and generation date in the metadata (e.g. *BananaProAI, YYYY-MM-DD*), archive the prompt and image in the repository (e.g. Zenodo), and apply a licence (recommended default: **CC BY 4.0**) if the image will be reused by others.

### Important note (applies to all tools)

- “You own the output” does **not** mean the image is “open” for everyone else to reuse.
- Others need a licence from you (e.g., **CC BY** or **CC0**), unless the work is non-copyrightable in their jurisdiction.

## 3) Third‑party rights checklist (before publishing/sharing)

Do not rely on “AI-generated” status as a shield. Avoid or get clearance for:

- recognisable **people** (privacy/publicity)
- **logos** and brand trade dress (trade marks)
- well-known **copyrighted characters** or highly distinctive franchise designs
- image-to-image using copyrighted inputs you do not have rights to

## 4) Make it citable (recommended)

Deposit the final image + metadata in **Zenodo** (or OSF/institutional repository) and obtain a **DOI**.

Upload:

- `image.png` (and/or SVG/PDF if vectorised)
- `README.md` with the metadata above
- optional: `prompt.txt`, `edit_log.txt`

Choose a licence:

- **Default: CC BY 4.0** (keeps attribution attached; best for academic reuse)
- **Use CC0** only when you explicitly do not care about attribution and want frictionless reuse.


## 5) Credit/citation templates (copy‑paste)

### A. Slides / talks (short credit line on-image)

Use the Zenodo DOI as the stable source.

- **Default (recommended):**  
  AI image (ChatGPT/OpenAI, YYYY). Zenodo: doi:10.5281/zenodo.XXXXXXX
- **If slides will be shared publicly (add licence):**  
  AI image (ChatGPT/OpenAI, YYYY), CC BY 4.0. Zenodo: doi:10.5281/zenodo.XXXXXXX
- **If you want explicit provenance (still short):**  
  Created by [Lab/Author] with ChatGPT (OpenAI), YYYY. Zenodo: doi:10.5281/zenodo.XXXXXXX

### B. Posters

- AI-generated illustration created with [tool/provider], YYYY; prompt and workflow archived on Zenodo (doi:…). Licence: CC BY 4.0.

### C. Papers / reports (figure caption note)

- **Note.** Illustration generated with **[tool/provider]**, **YYYY‑MM‑DD**; prompt and generation metadata available at **Zenodo (doi:…)**. Post-processing: **[x]**.

## 7) Integrity rule

AI images used in scientific outputs must be clearly **illustrative** unless explicitly labelled as synthetic/constructed content; never present AI imagery as an observation or measurement.

## Appendix: One-page checklist

- [ ] Metadata captured (tool, date, prompt, settings, edits)?
- [ ] Third‑party rights checked (people, logos, copyrighted characters, inputs)?
- [ ] Archived with a DOI (Zenodo/OSF/institutional repository)?
- [ ] Licence selected (CC BY 4.0 by default)?
- [ ] Correct credit line added for the medium (slides/posters/papers)?
